title: Hot Aisle Temperature Trending
dataSources: Hot aisle return air temperature sensors
spl:
index=environment sourcetype="sensor:temperature" position="hot_aisle"
| timechart span=1h avg(temp_f) as avg_temp by zone
| predict avg_temp as predicted future_timespan=7
description: Hot aisle trends indicate cooling efficiency and capacity margin. Rising trends signal approaching cooling limits.
value: Hot aisle trends indicate cooling efficiency and capacity margin. Rising trends signal approaching cooling limits.
implementation: Deploy sensors in hot aisle containment. Track return air temperatures. Compare hot aisle temps across zones to identify cooling imbalances. Use prediction to forecast capacity issues.
visualization: Line chart (hot aisle temps with prediction), Heatmap (zone × time), Bar chart (avg hot aisle by zone).
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Environmental sensors.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: Hot aisle return air temperature sensors.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Deploy sensors in hot aisle containment. Track return air temperatures. Compare hot aisle temps across zones to identify cooling imbalances. Use prediction to forecast capacity issues.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=environment sourcetype="sensor:temperature" position="hot_aisle"
| timechart span=1h avg(temp_f) as avg_temp by zone
| predict avg_temp as predicted future_timespan=7
```

Understanding this SPL

**Hot Aisle Temperature Trending** — Hot aisle trends indicate cooling efficiency and capacity margin. Rising trends signal approaching cooling limits.

Documented **Data sources**: Hot aisle return air temperature sensors. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Environmental sensors. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: environment; **sourcetype**: sensor:temperature. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=environment, sourcetype="sensor:temperature". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `timechart` plots the metric over time using **span=1h** buckets with a separate series **by zone** — ideal for trending and alerting on this use case.
• Pipeline stage (see **Hot Aisle Temperature Trending**): predict avg_temp as predicted future_timespan=7


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Line chart (hot aisle temps with prediction), Heatmap (zone × time), Bar chart (avg hot aisle by zone).
grandmaExplanation: Hot aisle trends indicate cooling efficiency and capacity margin — so power, cooling, and physical access problems are found before they hurt uptime.
